フリーランス2年目に入って、
「1年目と何が変わった？」と自分に聞いてみた👩‍💻

正直に答えると… 

1年目は「できるかな」
という不安が常にあった。
2年目は「どうやったらもっと良くなるか」
を考えられるようになってきた。

この視点の変化が、一番大きいかもしれない🥹✨

もちろん不安が0になったわけじゃないけど、
その不安と「うまく付き合える」
ようになってきた気がします☺️

フリーランス、続けてみると
見えてくることがある。
まだ始めるか迷っている方がいたら、
まずは一歩踏み出してみてほしいです💐✨

quoted_tweet.text:
ちょっとこれ見て…！

「GPT Images 2.0 × Shopify CLI × Codex（5.5）」を組み合わせ、コードは一行も書かず、半日でこのShopifyストアができました。

これはストア構築の常識がまた変わる。

Horizon的なノーコードブロック編集から、AIを使った「コードカスタマイズ」の価値が再評価されるはず。

やったことは以下です↓
-------------------------
1. まず全体のストア実装計画を立てる
2. GPT Images 2.0 で「素材画像」や「ページの完成見本」を作る
3. それをCodexやClaude Codeに渡して、DawnやRise系のテーマを直接カスタムしていく
-------------------------

このやり方だと、最終アウトプットの自由度が高いだけでなく、構築にかかる工数も桁違いに削減できます。

初回MTG時に、ほぼ完成した見本ストアを持っていって、「こんな感じでどうでしょう？💻（パカッ）」すら可能なスピード感。

Shopifyストアでこれができる日が来るとは...！

今週中に、具体的な手順をまとめた解説を公開します。
デモストアはスレッドから見れます👇

Claude Code is replacing your entire e-commerce team

Most stores die from operations (not ideas).

You need niche research, product creation, descriptions, images, listings, SEO, pricing, analytics (every single day).

But now one AI does it across Amazon Merch, Etsy Digital, Gumroad, Shopify Dropshipping and Redbubble.

> Turns one design into full optimized listings in seconds > Mines bestseller reviews and builds better versions > Generates digital products, mockups, sales copy > Builds entire Shopify stores from a prompt Scales the same idea across 5 platforms

You’re describing the workflow once and letting Claude execute.

Manual e-commerce is just donating time to people who automated it.

Bookmark before your competitor finishes the prompt.

kept.text:
I made a decision recently to stop working for  Nigerian companies, and wallahi, it’s one of the best decisions I’ve made.

This birthday message is from founder I work with in New York treats, he treat me like a son. He never misses salary, 20th to 25th, no stories. He appreciates every little effort and always reminds me how important I am to the company.

It’s crazy because it made me realize how much some of us were enduring before.

Nigeria founder with their stupid  800k and expect you to worship them, talk to you like you don’t matter, delay salary like it’s a favor, and still expect loyalty.

It’s not even about the money, it’s the lack of respect that really gets to you.

You won’t understand how draining it is until you experience better.

kept.text:
This guy literally explains how to connect Claude Code to a Shopify store.

Not theory. Actual setup.

Once connected, Claude can read product data, understand the catalog, generate descriptions, structure collections and help update the store without touching every product manually.

That used to be a $2k/month VA, a junior SEO guy and a content manager.

Now it’s slowly getting compressed into one terminal.

This is the real e-commerce shift.

Not AI writing copy.

AI running the boring ops layer behind the store.

The full article breaks down the whole system across Amazon Merch, Etsy, Gumroad and Shopify in more detail.

kept.text:
Bitrecs V2 is one of the more interesting subnet designs I’ve seen on Bittensor.

Most ecommerce recommendation engines rely on static models.

@Bitrecs is taking a different route.

Miners compete to build the best performing prompt artifact, validators score it on-chain, and the winning output gets deployed directly into live Shopify stores.

Then real shopper behavior feeds the next round.

So every transaction makes the system smarter.

Multi-LLM intelligence, decentralized inference through CHUTES, zero PII collected, and merchants already reporting up to 15% AOV lift.

This is the kind of AI x commerce use case that actually makes sense for Bittensor.

$TAO
SN122

stream of consciousness: 

it's never been more critical for your friends and family to have a side project.

do them a favor and get them started on one. get them a github repo, a codex/claude/lovable/replit/whatever. just a folder that they can work with an ai to commit and push stuff to.

the rate at which new tools and technology is both exhilarating and nauseating. the future has never been more unevenly distributed than it is today. however, what's different about today is that literally ~everyone can access it. the playing field has never been more even.

@tobi has this cool approach for being a futurist. it goes something like:

"predicting the future is easier than you think.

1) live in everyone's relative future, just use all the bleeding edge, latest tools to the max. just get the reps in.

2) when you compare that to what the average person is doing today, you have another data point to extrapolate a line from.

3) if on top of that, you're a student of history, you have many more data points to extrapolate a curve from."

it's pretty wild just how effective this thinking is. many of us do, attempt to do (1), every day. honestly, being able to learn about so many of the latest tools, approaches, etc is easily the greatest value add twitter/x has on my life. it's like the best group chat for being exposed to the future.

however, when i speak to non-technical friends & family, the same energy for all this new stuff isn't there. 

it's not because of lack of interest, if it was, it wouldn't really bother me. that said, i know these people and they would find it infinitely interesting and they would naturally love keeping up with this stuff.

what i'm learning is that most people don't have a low expectation, fun side project that they can apply all this new tech to.

when a new piece of tech comes out, people can intellectually understand that it's cool but if there's not an easy, personal thing to apply it to... then it just stays intellectually interesting and not viscerally exciting.

for example, there are all of those cool skills today like remotion/hyperframes/etc where if you point it at a repo, it'll create a fun marketing video. 

for anyone with a side project who sucks at video editing, this is super cool and you're itching to see what it produces for your project. 

however, if you don't, you're sorta like "that's cool" and then you move on. you don't feel inspired to try it, it feels too heavy, and you miss out on living in everyone's relative future.

why is it important to get them setup with a repo/software adjacent project?

ai's killer use case right now is that it truly democratizes software engineering. the latest models are as, if not  more, capable as most senior engineers we've ever worked with. the best tools are being made for agents that are software engineers at heart.md. 

you'll get the most bang for your buck by having something with a software component. get them setup with a repo somewhere and teach them how to ask their agent to point to tools, learn it, and see their eyes light up.

having something you're hacking on is fun but more importantly, it acts as an object to apply the future to.

it's something you care about, something you know, that you're building over time, and therefore when you encounter something that can push it forward in a way you could never imagine and at a speed you find neck-breaking, you can't help but find yourself sprinting to set it up.

helping your community feel that intoxicating feedback loop is one of the more valuable things you can do for them today.

How Claude Code is replacing the e-commerce operating system :

kept.text:
Live commerce is the next megatrend, and almost nobody is positioned for it.

I have been stalking one name...and I think it's setting up for a BIG move.

$SHOP

Here's why.

1) new emergence of "live commerce"
- whatnot is growing FAST and $shop joined their ecosystem last week
- shopify just unlocked live selling for millions of merchants
- live is outperforming traditional ecommerce
- billions in sales
- behavioral shift happening...

2) agentic commerce
- ceo of $shop: "agentic commerce could be one of the most exciting new paradigms…maybe since the internet"
- ai will browse + buy for users
- better conversion
- shopify can be the rails for this world

3) earnings catalyst
- multiple narratives hitting at once
- live commerce + ai + distribution expansion + payments network
- more live selling = more GMV = more payment volume

4) software signs of life
- seeing some life in software names like $team, $twlo 
- earnings pops on both of those^...why not $shop?

Timing...why now?
> live shopping integration JUST launched (not priced in fully yet)
> signs of life in software
> live commerce inflecting in US (already proven in china)
> agentic commerce narrative slowly emerging (still early)
> earnings catalyst
> charts look ready

Nobody has fully processed how big it can actually get.

$SHOP is now $127.
